narrative: Socrates expresses his gratitude to Theodorus for introducing him to both Theaetetus and the Eleatic Stranger. Theodorus replies that Socrates will owe him three times the thanks once the Stranger has completed his delineation of the Statesman and the Philosopher, having already finished with the Sophist. Socrates seizes on this, noting that Theodorus -- the great calculator and geometrician -- seems to rate all three figures at the same value, when in truth they are separated by an interval no geometrical ratio can express. Theodorus laughs at the hit, swearing by the god Ammon of Cyrene, and concedes the point, but turns the conversation forward: the Stranger should proceed to whichever inquiry he prefers, the Statesman or the Philosopher. The Stranger agrees he must continue what he has begun and not leave the work unfinished, but raises a practical question: shall they relieve Theaetetus and take his companion, Young Socrates, as the respondent instead? Theodorus approves, observing that the young always do better when they have intervals of rest.
refashioned: Having just completed their investigation of the Sophist, the group turns to the next question: what is the Statesman? Socrates teases Theodorus for treating the Sophist, the Statesman, and the Philosopher as equivalent figures -- as though a great mathematician would fail to see they are separated by immeasurable distances. Theodorus takes the joke in stride and asks the Stranger to continue with whichever inquiry he prefers. The Stranger agrees -- the work must not be left unfinished -- but suggests swapping in a fresh respondent. Young Socrates will take over for the tired Theaetetus.
elegant: The Sophist has been defined. Now the Stranger must pursue the Statesman. Socrates notes that no geometrical ratio can measure the distance between the Sophist, the Statesman, and the Philosopher -- they may share a surface resemblance, but they inhabit different orders of being. A fresh respondent, Young Socrates, steps in for the inquiry ahead.
summary: Following the investigation of the Sophist, the group agrees the Stranger should next define the Statesman. Young Socrates replaces Theaetetus as the respondent. Socrates observes that the Sophist, Statesman, and Philosopher are far more different from each other than they appear.
distilled: The Sophist, the Statesman, and the Philosopher may look alike on the surface, but no ratio can express the true distance between them.
notes: This dialogue is explicitly framed as a sequel to the Sophist, which itself followed the Theaetetus. All three dialogues share the same dramatic setting: a conversation in Athens involving Socrates, Theodorus of Cyrene (a mathematician), and an unnamed Stranger from Elea. The Stranger is associated with the Eleatic school of Parmenides and Zeno, known for rigorous logical analysis. Theodorus swears by Ammon, the Egyptian god worshipped at the oracle of Siwa in Cyrene (modern Libya), his home city. The planned trilogy (Sophist, Statesman, Philosopher) was never completed -- the Philosopher dialogue was either never written or has been lost. The method of division (diairesis) introduced in the Sophist will be carried forward as the primary analytical tool.

narrative: Socrates adds a personal note before stepping aside. He observes that both young men are related to him in some way: Theaetetus, the Stranger has remarked, bears the cut of Socrates' own ugly face, while the other simply shares his name. One should always be ready to recognize a kinsman by the quality of his conversation. Socrates himself examined Theaetetus yesterday and has just been listening to his answers; his namesake he has not yet tested, but another time will serve. For today, let Young Socrates answer the Stranger.
refashioned: Before stepping back, Socrates notes a personal connection to both young men. Theaetetus resembles him physically -- the famous Socratic ugliness -- while Young Socrates shares his name. The real test of kinship, though, is conversation. Socrates has already examined Theaetetus; now it is his namesake's turn to be tested through dialogue.
elegant: Socrates claims kinship with both young men -- one by face, the other by name. But the true test of family resemblance is how a person thinks. Having examined Theaetetus, he hands his namesake over to the Stranger's questioning.
summary: Socrates notes his connection to both young men and steps aside, having already examined Theaetetus. He leaves Young Socrates to be tested by the Stranger in the investigation of the Statesman.
distilled: The truest kinship is intellectual -- recognized not by appearance or name, but by the quality of one's thinking.
notes: The reference to Socrates' ugliness (snub nose, bulging eyes) appears in multiple dialogues, notably the Theaetetus and the Symposium (where Alcibiades compares him to a Silenus statue). Young Socrates (Socrates the Younger) was a historical figure, a student in Plato's circle who also appears briefly in the Theaetetus. He should not be confused with the elder Socrates. The remark about recognizing kinsmen through conversation reflects the Socratic conviction that philosophical dialogue reveals character more reliably than any external marker.

narrative: The Stranger turns to Young Socrates and confirms he has heard the elder Socrates' proposal. Young Socrates agrees to serve as respondent. The Stranger then sets the terms of the inquiry: after the Sophist, the Statesman naturally follows next in order. He asks whether the Statesman should be ranked among those who possess knowledge. Young Socrates says yes. The Stranger notes that the sciences must be divided as before -- meaning through the same method of division used in the Sophist -- but the division will not fall at the same point. The cut will come somewhere different this time. Young Socrates agrees, and the inquiry is ready to begin.
refashioned: With Young Socrates confirmed as respondent, the Stranger frames the investigation. The Statesman, like the Sophist, must be defined through systematic division of knowledge. But though the method is the same, the dividing lines will fall differently. The Sophist was found by cutting through one set of distinctions; the Statesman will require different ones. The question that launches the inquiry: does the Statesman possess a form of knowledge? Yes -- and so the task is to locate exactly which kind.
elegant: The method is the same: divide knowledge systematically until the target stands alone. But the cuts will fall differently for the Statesman than they did for the Sophist. The starting premise is that statesmanship is a form of knowledge -- the question is which form, and how it differs from every other.
summary: The Stranger establishes that the Statesman will be defined using the same method of division as the Sophist, but with different dividing points. The key premise is that statesmanship is a form of knowledge.
distilled: The same method of division that revealed the Sophist will now be turned on the Statesman, but the cuts through knowledge will fall at entirely different points.
notes: The method of division (diairesis) is Plato's systematic technique of classification: begin with a broad category and repeatedly divide it into two until the target definition emerges. It was introduced formally in the Phaedrus (265d-266b) and practiced extensively in the Sophist. The Stranger's insistence that the Statesman belongs among 'those who have science' (episteme) reflects the Socratic-Platonic commitment to the idea that genuine governance requires knowledge, not merely power or popular support -- a theme central to the Republic and the Gorgias.

narrative:
The Stranger poses the opening question: where shall they discover the path of the Statesman? They must find it, separate it off, and mark all diverging paths with different classifications, so that the mind can conceive of all knowledge under two primary classes. Young Socrates defers -- finding the path is the Stranger's business. The Stranger corrects him: the discovery, once made, must belong to them both.

The first division is between theoretical and practical knowledge. Arithmetic and its kindred arts are purely abstract -- knowledge separated entirely from action. But in carpentry and the other handicrafts, the workman's knowledge is inseparable from his making; he produces things that did not previously exist. So the sciences divide into those that are purely intellectual and those that are practical.

The Stranger then asks a crucial question: are the statesman, the king, the master of a household, and the slave-master all the same thing, or does each name correspond to a different art? He reframes the point with an analogy: if a private citizen has the medical knowledge to advise a public physician, we call that citizen a physician regardless of his official position. Likewise, if a private citizen possesses the knowledge a ruler ought to have, he deserves to be called royal in reference to his art, whether or not he actually holds power. The science of a true king is royal science. And since a large household is simply a small state, household management and political rule differ not in kind but only in scale.
refashioned:
The Stranger launches the investigation with a fundamental question: what kind of knowledge does the Statesman possess? To answer it, they must divide all knowledge into categories and trace the Statesman's art to its proper location.

The first cut separates theoretical knowledge from practical knowledge. Arithmetic judges and calculates but produces nothing physical. Carpentry, by contrast, embeds knowledge in the act of making -- the craftsman's understanding is inseparable from the objects he creates. All sciences fall on one side or the other: pure intellect or hands-on production.

Where does the Statesman fall? The Stranger presses further with a key insight: the titles of king, statesman, master, and household manager all refer to the same underlying expertise. If a private citizen has the knowledge to advise a physician, we call that person a physician. If a private citizen understands what a ruler needs to know, that person possesses royal science whether or not they hold office. Knowledge defines the role, not the title. And governing a large household is no different in kind from governing a small state -- they differ only in scale.
elegant: All knowledge divides into the theoretical and the practical -- what the mind grasps versus what the hands produce. The Statesman's art belongs to the mind. And here is the deeper point: the knowledge that makes someone a ruler is the same whether they govern a household or a nation, whether they hold office or advise from private life. The title is irrelevant. What matters is whether you possess the science of rule.
summary: The Stranger divides all knowledge into theoretical and practical, placing the Statesman's art on the theoretical side. He then argues that king, statesman, and household manager all exercise the same fundamental expertise, differing only in scale, not in kind.
distilled: The art of ruling is a form of knowledge, not a function of office -- whoever possesses it is a ruler in truth, whether they govern a household or a nation.
notes: The theoretical/practical division of knowledge (episteme gnostike vs. episteme praktike) is a foundational move in Platonic epistemology. It will be refined further in this dialogue. The claim that household management (oikonomia) and political rule (politike) are the same art at different scales was a common Greek assumption, articulated by Xenophon in his Oeconomicus and discussed critically by Aristotle in Politics I.1-2, where Aristotle explicitly rejects this identification. The argument that knowledge rather than office defines the ruler echoes Republic 473c-d, where Plato argues that philosophers must become kings or kings become philosophers. The analogy of the private physician parallels Gorgias 464b-465a.

narrative:
The Stranger recaps: there is one science common to all these roles -- call it royal, political, or economical, the name does not matter. What matters is its nature. A king maintains his rule not primarily through physical labor or bodily strength but through the intelligence and strength of his mind. Therefore the king belongs on the intellectual side of the original division, not the practical.

With this settled, statesmanship and kingship collapse into a single art: the royal science of intellectual command. The next task is to divide the sphere of knowledge further. The Stranger asks Young Socrates to find a joint or natural division point within theoretical knowledge.

Consider arithmetic, the Stranger suggests. It is clearly a science of knowledge. Its function is to discern and judge differences among numbers -- and nothing more. It passes judgment but does not issue orders. Now consider the master-builder: he too contributes knowledge rather than manual labor, and so shares in theoretical science. But unlike the arithmetician, the master-builder does not stop at forming a judgment. He must assign tasks to individual workmen and direct their labor until the work is complete.

Here is the dividing line within theoretical knowledge: some sciences have the power of judging only, while others both judge and command. Pure judgment on one side, directive authority on the other.
refashioned:
Having established that statesmanship is a theoretical rather than practical art, the Stranger now divides theoretical knowledge itself.

Some forms of knowledge only judge. Arithmetic evaluates the relationships between numbers and stops there -- it pronounces verdicts but gives no orders. Other forms of knowledge judge and then command. The master-builder, like the arithmetician, works with his mind rather than his hands. But unlike the arithmetician, the master-builder cannot simply declare his conclusions and walk away. He must assign specific tasks to specific workers and oversee them until the project is finished.

This is the crucial division within theoretical knowledge: judging versus commanding. The pure judge evaluates and stops. The commander evaluates and then directs action based on that evaluation. The Statesman clearly belongs on the commanding side.
elegant: Within theoretical knowledge lies a decisive split. The arithmetician judges -- evaluates, distinguishes, pronounces -- and stops. The master-builder also judges, but then he commands: assigning tasks, directing workers, overseeing completion. One art stops at the verdict. The other carries the verdict into action through authority over others. The Statesman's knowledge is of the second kind.
summary: Theoretical knowledge divides into two types: the purely evaluative (like arithmetic, which only judges) and the directive (like architecture, which judges and then commands workers). Statesmanship belongs to the directive class -- knowledge that both evaluates and issues orders.
distilled: The difference between a judge and a ruler is that one stops at the verdict while the other carries it into action through command.
notes: The distinction between critical/judging knowledge (gnostike kritike) and directive/commanding knowledge (gnostike epitaktike) is original to this dialogue and becomes one of its most important conceptual contributions. The master-builder (architekton) example is significant -- the Greek word literally means 'chief craftsman' and is the root of 'architect.' Plato uses it because the architect occupies an intermediate position: clearly intellectual (not manual labor) yet clearly directive (not purely contemplative). Aristotle later picks up this distinction in Metaphysics I.1, ranking the architect above the manual laborer precisely because he knows the 'why' and not merely the 'that.'

narrative:
The Stranger confirms the division: all knowledge has two branches -- one that rules, and one that merely judges. Young Socrates agrees, and the Stranger notes that when two people are working together on a shared inquiry, being of one mind matters more than worrying about what outsiders might think of their terminology.

Now the question: in which division does the king belong? Is he a judge, a kind of spectator who evaluates? Or does he exercise the art of command? Clearly the latter -- he is a ruler. But the art of command itself admits further division. The Stranger draws an analogy between manufacturers and retail dealers. The retailer receives goods that others have produced and sells them secondhand. Similarly, a herald receives commands from above and transmits them to others below. The herald commands, but he commands with borrowed authority -- he passes along orders that originated elsewhere, just as the retailer sells goods he did not make.

Shall they then place the king in the same class as heralds, interpreters, boatswains, prophets, and all others who merely relay commands? Or should they recognize a distinct class: those who command on their own authority, who are, as it were, rulers-for-themselves?
refashioned:
The king belongs to the commanding branch of knowledge, not the judging branch. But commanding itself needs further division.

Consider the difference between a manufacturer and a retailer. The manufacturer creates original goods; the retailer merely resells what others have produced. The same distinction applies to authority. A herald receives orders from a superior and passes them along -- he exercises command, but it is secondhand command, borrowed authority. Prophets, interpreters, and naval officers often function the same way: they transmit directives that originated with someone else.

The true ruler does not relay borrowed commands. He issues original directives from his own judgment. This is the difference between derivative authority and sovereign authority -- between those who pass along someone else's decisions and those who generate the decisions themselves. The king belongs in the latter class: the commander-for-himself.
elegant: Not all command is equal. A herald relays orders from above. A retailer sells goods someone else made. Both exercise a kind of authority, but it is borrowed -- secondhand power. The true ruler commands from his own judgment, not from someone else's script. The distinction is between derivative authority and sovereign authority, and the Statesman stands on the sovereign side.
summary: The art of command divides further into derivative command (like a herald relaying orders from above) and original command (issuing directives from one's own authority). The true ruler commands on his own authority, not as a relay for someone else's decisions.
distilled: The difference between a herald and a king is the difference between passing along someone else's orders and originating your own.
notes: The manufacturer/retailer analogy (autopoles/kapelos) reflects the Greek economic distinction between the producer who sells his own goods and the middleman who trades in others' goods. This had moral overtones in Greek thought -- Aristotle in Politics I.9-10 treats retail trade (kapeleia) as an inferior, potentially corrupting form of commerce. The herald (keryx) was an important figure in Greek political and military life, serving as an official messenger between cities, armies, and assemblies. Prophets (manteis) in Greek religion often served as interpreters of divine will rather than originators of it -- they relay the gods' commands, making them analogous to heralds. The term 'rulers-for-themselves' (autepitaktike) is coined here by the Stranger.

narrative: The Stranger crystallizes the point. They should not lump the king together with heralds, interpreters, boatswains, prophets, and the many kindred officials who all exercise command under someone else's authority. The class of supreme rulers -- those who rule for themselves -- is so rarely distinguished that it almost lacks a name. Following the analogy of manufacturers who sell for themselves versus retailers who sell others' goods, they should refer kings to a supreme or self-ruling science, leaving the subordinate commanders to be classified separately. The inquiry is about the ruler, not about those who merely transmit the ruler's will.
refashioned: The king must not be confused with the many officials who exercise delegated authority -- heralds, interpreters, military officers, prophets. These all command, but on someone else's behalf. The class of genuine, sovereign rulers is so rarely isolated in thought that it barely has a name. Yet this is precisely the class the inquiry seeks: those who command from their own knowledge and on their own authority. Everything else -- the entire apparatus of delegated command -- can be set aside.
elegant: The world is full of officials who command on someone else's behalf. The class of those who command from their own authority is so rare it barely has a name. That nameless class is what the inquiry seeks.
summary: The Stranger distinguishes the king from all officials who exercise delegated authority. The true ruler belongs to a nearly nameless class: those who command from their own knowledge rather than relaying someone else's orders.
distilled: True sovereignty is so rare that the class of those who genuinely rule from their own authority barely has a name.
notes: The observation that 'self-ruling science' (autepitaktike) is 'almost nameless' reflects a recurring Platonic concern with the inadequacy of ordinary language to capture philosophical distinctions. In the Sophist (218c), the Stranger similarly notes the difficulty of naming things that common usage has not distinguished. The list of subordinate commanders -- heralds, interpreters, boatswains, prophets -- represents the typical command structure of a Greek city-state, where many officials exercised authority delegated from the assembly, the council, or the generals.

narrative: Young Socrates approves the distinction. The Stranger summarizes: a clear line has been drawn between the person who gives his own commands and the person who transmits another's. Now the question is whether the supreme power -- the self-commanding art -- permits any further division. Young Socrates is ready to proceed and asks where the next cut should be made.
refashioned: With the distinction between sovereign and delegated command firmly established, the question becomes whether sovereign command itself can be divided further. The Stranger believes it can. The next cut in the analysis is about to be made.
elegant: Sovereign command has been isolated from its many imitators. Now the question sharpens: can this supreme art itself be divided further?
summary: Having separated sovereign command from delegated command, the Stranger prepares to divide sovereign command itself into further categories.
distilled: Once you have isolated genuine authority from its many counterfeits, the real work of definition begins.
notes: This brief transitional passage signals the move from Chapter 2 (The Art of Ruling) to Chapter 3 (The King as Herdsman), where the method of division will be applied to the objects over which the ruler commands rather than to the nature of command itself. The structure mirrors the Sophist, where each successful division opens onto a new question requiring further division.

narrative:
The Stranger proposes the next principle of division: all rulers command for the sake of producing or managing something. The things they oversee can be divided into those that have life and those that are without life. This gives them two branches of directive knowledge: one governing the production of lifeless objects, the other governing living beings.

They set aside the lifeless branch immediately, since the royal science is clearly not like the art of a master-workman presiding over inert materials. The king has a nobler function: the management and control of living beings.

Within the management of living things, there is a further distinction: tending individuals versus tending groups. The statesman is not like a groom caring for a single horse; he is more like the keeper of a drove of horses or a herd of oxen. His art concerns the collective management of living creatures -- the management of herds. Young Socrates grasps the point, and the Stranger is satisfied with whatever name suggests itself for this art of tending many animals together.
refashioned:
The next division concerns what the ruler's command is directed toward. All command aims at producing or managing something, and those objects divide into the living and the non-living.

The non-living side -- engineering, construction, manufacturing -- can be set aside immediately. The Statesman does not preside over inert materials. His domain is the management of living beings.

But not all management of living beings is the same. A groom tends a single horse. The Statesman, by contrast, manages groups -- herds, flocks, populations. His art is collective management, not individual care. The relevant analogy is not the horse-trainer but the herdsman: someone responsible for the welfare and direction of an entire group of living creatures.
elegant: The ruler's domain is living beings, not lifeless materials. And within that domain, the Statesman is not the groom of a single horse but the herdsman of a flock -- his art is collective management, the direction of groups rather than individuals.
summary: The Stranger divides the objects of command into living and non-living, placing the Statesman on the living side. He then distinguishes individual care from collective management, identifying the Statesman as a herdsman of groups rather than a tender of individuals.
distilled: The Statesman's art is the collective management of living beings -- he is a herdsman, not a groom.
notes: The herdsman analogy (poimenike) for political rule was widespread in Greek thought. Homer's standard epithet for Agamemnon is 'shepherd of the people' (poimen laon). The analogy will be central to this dialogue and will ultimately be subjected to serious criticism. The distinction between tending individuals and tending groups maps onto a broader Greek distinction between private arts (like medicine, which treats one patient at a time) and political arts (which manage populations). Xenophon's Cyropaedia and Memorabilia both develop the ruler-as-herdsman comparison extensively.

narrative: The Stranger commends Young Socrates for not being too particular about names -- a trait that will make him wiser in old age. Setting aside the question of terminology, the Stranger asks whether the art of herding can be divided into two kinds, which would narrow their search by half. Young Socrates tries: one kind of herding manages humans, the other manages beasts. The Stranger acknowledges this is a straightforward and confident division, but warns that Young Socrates has fallen into an error they should learn to avoid.
refashioned: Young Socrates attempts the next division: herd management splits into the management of humans and the management of animals. The Stranger acknowledges the confidence of this move but flags it as an error. Dividing 'humans versus everything else' feels natural, but it is methodologically flawed. The Stranger will explain why -- and the explanation will reveal one of the most important principles of the entire method of division.
elegant: Young Socrates' instinct is to divide herds into humans and animals. It feels obvious. But the obvious cut is often the wrong one, and the Stranger warns that this error contains a lesson more valuable than the answer itself.
summary: Young Socrates divides herding into human management and animal management. The Stranger warns this seemingly natural division commits a methodological error that must be understood and avoided.
distilled: The most obvious way to carve up a category is often the most misleading.
notes: The Stranger's praise of Young Socrates for being 'not too particular about names' (me lian akribologoumenos peri ta onomata) reflects a pragmatic approach to philosophical inquiry: definitions matter more than labels. This contrasts with the Cratylus, where the relationship between names and things is explored at length. The error Young Socrates commits -- dividing by singling out one species and lumping everything else together -- will be diagnosed in the following passages as a violation of proper classificatory method.

narrative: The Stranger explains the error. In dividing a class, one should not cut off a single small portion from many larger portions. The part separated must itself be a genuine species -- a natural kind, not merely a convenient label. Young Socrates divided by isolating humans and lumping all other animals together as 'beasts,' but this is like chipping off a small piece rather than cutting through the middle. The safer and more productive method is always to cut through the middle of a class, dividing it into roughly equal halves, because this is far more likely to reveal genuine natural kinds. Attention to this principle makes all the difference in the process of inquiry.
refashioned:
The error is this: Young Socrates isolated one small group (humans) and lumped everything else together (all other animals). This is not a proper division -- it is just chipping off a piece. A valid division must cut a class into genuine species, not separate one fragment from an undifferentiated remainder.

The principle is: always divide through the middle. When you cut a category into roughly equal halves, you are far more likely to find natural kinds -- real joints in the structure of things. When you chip off one small piece because you already know where you want to end up, you skip over the real structure and arrive at your destination without understanding the terrain. This principle is what separates rigorous classification from mere labeling.
elegant: Do not chip off a small piece and call the remainder a class. Cut through the middle. The goal of division is not to reach your answer quickly but to find the real joints in the structure of things. Haste in classification is a guarantee of error.
summary: The Stranger articulates a core principle of proper division: always cut through the middle of a class into genuine species rather than chipping off one small portion and treating the remainder as a single kind. This yields natural classifications rather than artificial ones.
distilled: The purpose of division is to find the natural joints in reality, and this requires cutting through the middle of categories rather than chipping off whatever piece you already want.
notes: This passage articulates the key methodological principle of the Statesman: division must follow natural joints (kat' arthra), not convenience. The metaphor of 'cutting at the joints' appears explicitly in the Phaedrus (265e), where Socrates compares the dialectician to a butcher who carves along natural articulations rather than hacking through bone. The principle of 'dividing through the middle' (meson temnein) is an innovation of this dialogue -- the Sophist employed division but did not theorize about optimal cutting strategy. This passage has been influential in the philosophy of classification and natural kinds.

narrative: Young Socrates asks the Stranger to explain more clearly. The Stranger promises to try, out of appreciation for Young Socrates' abilities, though he admits he cannot fully explain himself at present. The full meaning will become clearer as they proceed. Young Socrates presses: what exactly was the error in their recent division?
refashioned: Young Socrates asks for clarification. The Stranger promises the full meaning will emerge as the inquiry continues -- the error will be easier to understand once they see what a correct division looks like by contrast.
elegant: The principle will become clear through practice. Seeing what proper division looks like will illuminate why the earlier cut was wrong.
summary: Young Socrates asks for further clarification of the error. The Stranger promises the principle will become clearer as the inquiry progresses and they practice correct division.
distilled: Some principles of method can only be understood by watching them work in practice.
notes: The Stranger's patience and pedagogical sensitivity here is notable. Rather than lecturing abstractly, he promises to demonstrate the principle through the inquiry itself -- a teaching method consistent with the Socratic tradition of learning through guided discovery rather than passive instruction. The phrase 'out of love to your good parts' (dia ten sen euphyian) suggests that philosophical aptitude is recognized through the quality of one's errors as much as through one's correct answers.

narrative:
The Stranger illustrates the error with vivid examples. Suppose someone wanted to divide the human race and did so the way people do in Greece: Hellenes on one side, and all other peoples -- innumerable, with no shared language or customs -- lumped together as 'barbarians.' Because they share a single name, they are treated as a single species. But this is absurd. It would be like dividing all numbers by cutting off ten thousand as one class and grouping all the rest under a different name -- just because you gave them one label does not make them one kind.

A far better division of numbers would be into odd and even. A far better division of the human race would be into male and female. One should only separate off a specific group like the Lydians or Phrygians when no further division into genuine classes is possible -- when one has reached the level of actual species, not before.
refashioned:
The Stranger gives the key example. Greeks divide humanity into 'Hellenes' and 'barbarians' -- one specific group on one side, and every other people on earth lumped together on the other. Because the remainder shares a single label ('barbarian'), it gets treated as a single kind. But sharing a name does not make something a species. You could divide all numbers by putting ten thousand on one side and everything else on the other, but that tells you nothing about the structure of numbers. The real division of numbers is odd versus even. The real division of the human race is male versus female.

The lesson: never separate off a particular group until you have exhausted all the genuine structural divisions. Isolating one species and calling the remainder a class is the fundamental error of lazy classification.
elegant: Greeks call themselves 'Hellenes' and everyone else 'barbarians,' as if a shared label made countless unrelated peoples a single species. This is the error of lazy classification: treating a leftover pile as a natural kind. The real division of numbers is odd and even, not 'ten thousand and everything else.' The real division of humanity is male and female, not 'us and everyone else.'
summary: The Stranger illustrates the error with the Greek division of humanity into Hellenes and barbarians: lumping unrelated peoples under one name does not make them a genuine class. Proper division follows structural features (like odd/even in numbers) rather than isolating one group and treating the remainder as a single kind.
distilled: A shared label does not make a real class -- calling everyone who is not Greek a 'barbarian' tells you nothing about the actual structure of humanity.
notes: The Hellene/barbarian example is philosophically significant: Plato is critiquing Greek ethnocentrism through the lens of logical method. This argument was later taken up directly by Aristotle in Politics I.2 and III.14. The Lydians were a people of western Anatolia (modern Turkey) whose last king, Croesus, was famous for his wealth and dealings with the Greeks. The Phrygians occupied central Anatolia and were known to the Greeks through mythology (Midas, Gordius) and through the cult of Cybele. The odd/even division of numbers was fundamental to Pythagorean mathematics and philosophy, where odd and even were considered basic principles of reality.

narrative:
Young Socrates asks for the distinction between a part and a class to be made still plainer. The Stranger resists -- they have already digressed too far from the main inquiry and should not wander further. But he offers one crucial clarification: a class is necessarily a part, but a part is not necessarily a class. Having a name and being a portion of something does not automatically make that portion a genuine species. This is the principle Young Socrates should remember.

The Stranger then returns to where they went wrong. The question was how to divide the management of herds, and Young Socrates jumped too quickly to the answer that there were two species: humans and all brutes. Taking away 'humans' as a part, he assumed the remainder ('brutes') formed a genuine class simply because it had a common name. The Stranger illustrates the absurdity with a striking image: suppose a crane, being wise and proud, divided all animals into cranes and non-cranes, lumping humans in with every other creature under the label 'brutes.' The crane would be committing exactly the same error of classification that Young Socrates committed. To avoid this error, they must not divide the whole class of animals at once by singling out humans; the first division produced the source of their mistake.
refashioned:
Young Socrates wants the distinction between a 'part' and a 'class' spelled out. The Stranger provides the essential principle: every class is a part, but not every part is a class. Just because you can carve off a piece and give it a name does not make it a genuine species.

This is exactly what went wrong. Young Socrates divided herding into 'human management' and 'animal management,' treating 'all other animals' as a single class just because they share the label 'brutes.' But imagine a crane doing the same thing: cranes on one side, everything else -- humans included -- on the other, all lumped together as 'brutes.' The crane would be just as wrong as the Greek who calls everyone a barbarian.

The fix: do not begin by singling out the group you are looking for. Divide the whole class at its natural joints, even if this means the target species does not emerge until several divisions later.
elegant: Every class is a part, but not every part is a class. Imagine a crane dividing all animals into 'cranes' and 'non-cranes' -- humans lumped in with the rest as brutes. It would be committing exactly the same error as the human who separates 'humans' from 'all other animals.' The antidote is patience: divide at natural joints, even if the species you seek does not appear until the final cut.
summary: The Stranger clarifies that not every part of a category is a genuine class. He illustrates the error with the image of a crane classifying all non-cranes as 'brutes,' showing that singling out one species and lumping the rest together is always methodologically wrong, no matter who does it.
distilled: A crane dividing all animals into cranes and non-cranes would be committing the same error as a human who divides them into humans and brutes.
notes: The crane example is one of Plato's most memorable illustrations and has been widely discussed in the history of philosophy and biology. It anticipates modern concerns about anthropocentrism in taxonomy. The part/class (meros/eidos) distinction is a significant contribution to the theory of classification: Plato is arguing that a legitimate taxonomic group must be defined by shared positive features, not merely by exclusion from another group. This principle influenced Aristotle's biological classifications in the Parts of Animals and History of Animals. The Stranger's reluctance to pursue the theoretical point further -- 'we have already digressed further than we ought' -- reflects the dialogue's self-conscious attention to methodological pacing.

narrative:
The Stranger begins the corrected division. Returning to their earlier point -- that the commanding branch of knowledge concerned the rearing of living creatures in herds -- the Stranger notes that a prior division was already implicit: animals divide into tame and wild. Those whose nature can be tamed are called tame; those that cannot be tamed are called wild. Political science concerns itself with tame animals, and specifically with tame gregarious animals.

But this time they must not rush. They should not divide by taking the whole class at once. Haste brought the misfortune mentioned in the proverb: too much haste is too little speed. Young Socrates accepts the rebuke cheerfully.

The Stranger starts fresh with the collective rearing of tame gregarious animals and introduces a new division: water herds versus land herds. The examples are vivid -- fish preserves in the Nile, ponds of the Great King of Persia, goose and crane nurseries in the plains of Thessaly. These all represent the management of water-dwelling herds. But the royal art obviously belongs to the management of land herds, not water herds. No one needs to be told on which side the Statesman falls.
refashioned:
The Stranger begins the corrected division, this time following the method properly.

First, animals divide into tame and wild. Political science concerns only tame animals -- and specifically tame animals that live in groups. Now the task is to subdivide this category without rushing to isolate humans.

The first cut: water herds versus land herds. Fish farms in the Nile, the royal ponds of the Persian King, goose and crane nurseries on the Thessalian plains -- all of these represent the management of water-dwelling herds. The Statesman obviously belongs to the management of land herds. That much requires no argument.
elegant: Tame, gregarious animals divide into water herds and land herds. Fish farms, goose nurseries, and royal ponds fall on one side. The Statesman's domain falls on the other. This much is obvious -- the real work of division lies ahead.
summary: The Stranger restarts the division properly: tame gregarious animals divide first into water herds and land herds. The Statesman clearly belongs to the management of land herds. The Stranger emphasizes proceeding carefully rather than rushing to the answer.
distilled: Haste in classification is too much speed and too little progress -- the only way forward is patient, systematic division.
notes: The Persian King's fish preserves and animal parks (paradeisoi, from which we get the word 'paradise') were well known to the Greeks through Xenophon's accounts in the Anabasis and Cyropaedia. The Thessalian plains in northern Greece were famous for horse-breeding and agriculture. The proverb 'more haste, less speed' (speude bradeos) was proverbial in Greek -- it was later attributed to Augustus Caesar as 'festina lente.' The division into tame and wild (hemera/agria) maps onto the Greek cultural distinction between civilization and nature, between the polis and the wild.

narrative:
The division of land herds continues. The Stranger divides them into those that fly and those that walk. The political animal is clearly a pedestrian -- a walker, not a flyer. Even a fool would know that.

Now the art of managing walking animals must be divided further, as one might halve an even number. The Stranger notes that two paths appear: one shorter, cutting off a small piece and leaving a large remainder, and one longer, which better follows the principle of dividing through the middle. Young Socrates wants to try both, and the Stranger agrees to take them in turn, starting with the longer, more methodical route.

The tame walking herding animals divide into those that grow horns and those without horns. Rather than inventing complex names, they simply assign one branch of management to horned herds and the other to hornless herds. The king is clearly the shepherd of the hornless, polled herd.
refashioned:
Land herds divide into flyers and walkers. The Statesman governs walkers -- no argument needed.

Walking animals divide into horned and hornless. Two analytical paths present themselves: a short cut that would isolate humans quickly, or the longer route that divides through the middle as proper method requires. The Stranger takes the longer route.

Horned herds include cattle, goats, and deer. The Statesman governs the hornless herd. The classification narrows steadily: from living things to tame animals, to gregarious tame animals, to land herds, to walkers, to the hornless among them.
elegant: From all living things down to tame gregarious land-walking hornless animals -- each cut narrows the field by half and follows the natural joints. Two paths were available: the quick shortcut and the methodical route. The Stranger chose method over speed, because proper classification is its own reward.
summary: The Stranger continues dividing: land animals split into flyers and walkers, then walkers into horned and hornless. Following proper method, the longer and more systematic path is chosen over the shortcut. The Statesman is identified as the shepherd of hornless herds.
distilled: Each proper division halves the field and follows a natural joint, steadily narrowing the classification from all living things toward the Statesman's specific domain.
notes: The two paths -- the shorter (cutting off a small piece) and the longer (dividing through the middle) -- illustrate the Stranger's methodological principle in action. He deliberately chooses the longer route to model proper technique, even when a shortcut would reach the same result. The distinction between horned (kerata echon) and hornless (akeraton) animals was a standard Greek zoological classification. The term 'polled' (kolobon) refers specifically to animals naturally lacking horns. This taxonomic approach anticipates Aristotle's biological classifications in the Parts of Animals, where horns are among the distinguishing features discussed.

narrative:
The hornless herd must be further divided. The Stranger considers several possible bases for division: cloven-footed versus non-cloven, or mixing breeds versus keeping breeds pure. He explains the second option: horses and asses naturally breed with one another, producing mules, while the rest of the hornless tame herd does not interbreed. The Statesman has charge of the unmixed, pure-bred race.

This group must be divided once more. Nearly every tame gregarious animal has now been accounted for, with only two species remaining -- dogs being excluded since they are not truly gregarious. To distinguish the final two, the Stranger reaches for a mathematical metaphor, fitting for Young Socrates the geometry student: man walks as a biped, whose power is two feet -- like a diagonal whose square is two. The remaining kind walks on four feet -- whose power is twice two, like the diagonal of the diagonal.

The Stranger observes with amusement that in this classification, human beings have ended up running in the same class as the freest and airiest of creatures. And one would expect the slowest runner to arrive last in any race.
refashioned:
The hornless herd divides again. Some hornless animals interbreed across species -- horses and asses produce mules. The remainder keep to their own kind. The Statesman governs the pure-bred, non-interbreeding group.

Only two species remain, and the Stranger uses a geometrical joke to distinguish them, playing to Young Socrates' mathematical training. Humans are bipeds -- their 'power' is two feet, like a square root of two. The other remaining species are quadrupeds -- their power is the square root of four. Humans, it turns out, have been classified alongside birds: both are bipeds, both featherless, running in the same taxonomic lane. The Stranger finds this amusing -- the king has ended up sharing a classification with the most nimble creatures in nature.
elegant: Through patient division, humans and birds land in the same class: both bipeds among the hornless, pure-bred, tame, gregarious, walking, land-dwelling animals. The king finds himself racing alongside the bird-catcher. This is what happens when you follow the logic of classification honestly -- it does not flatter human dignity.
summary: Further division separates interbreeding animals from pure-bred ones, then uses a mathematical distinction between bipeds and quadrupeds. Humans end up classified alongside birds as bipeds, producing the comic result that the king shares a category with the bird-catcher.
distilled: Honest classification does not flatter -- follow the logic far enough and humans end up in the same category as birds.
notes: The mathematical metaphor uses the concept of 'dunamis' (power/square) from Greek geometry. The diagonal of a unit square has a length of the square root of 2 (an irrational number), which was a famous discovery of the Pythagorean school and reportedly caused a philosophical crisis. The reference to the Meno connects to that dialogue's geometric demonstration (82b-85b) where Socrates uses the diagonal of a square to illustrate the theory of recollection. The joke about humans and birds sharing a classification anticipates Diogenes the Cynic's famous response to Plato's definition of man as a 'featherless biped': Diogenes reportedly plucked a chicken and declared 'Here is Plato's man!' This anecdote, reported by Diogenes Laertius (VI.40), may be a direct response to this passage.

narrative: The Stranger continues the joke: the result is even more ridiculous than it appears. The king is found running alongside the herd, in close competition with the bird-catcher -- who of all people is most expert at the airy, birdlike life. The classification has produced an absurd pairing, placing the ruler of humanity in the same class as the trapper of birds.
refashioned: The absurdity deepens. Through this chain of divisions, the king has ended up classified alongside the bird-catcher -- the person whose entire expertise is catching the very creatures humans were just grouped with. The method of division does not care about dignity or social rank; it follows the logical structure of categories wherever it leads, even to comically deflating results.
elegant: The king ends up racing the bird-catcher. The method of division has no respect for human vanity -- it classifies by structure, not by status.
summary: The chain of divisions produces the comic result of classifying the king alongside the bird-catcher, illustrating that the method of division follows logical structure rather than social importance.
distilled: Rigorous classification follows structural logic, not human vanity -- which is why the king ends up next to the bird-catcher.
notes: The parenthetical editorial note in the original text reflects an ancient scholarly debate about whether this passage refers to the biped/bird classification (placing the king alongside the bird-catcher) or to an alternative reading involving quadrupeds (placing the king alongside the pig-driver). The bird-catcher (ixeutes) interpretation is generally preferred by modern scholars. The humor serves a philosophical purpose: it demonstrates that the method of division is objective and impersonal, a point the Stranger will make explicit in the next passage.

narrative:
Young Socrates asks for the lesson. The Stranger draws it out: this result demonstrates a principle already established in the inquiry about the Sophist -- the dialectical method is no respecter of persons. It does not elevate the great above the small but always arrives at the truest result in its own way, regardless of what is flattering or convenient.

With the principle affirmed, the Stranger offers to take Young Socrates by the shorter road to the definition of the king, without waiting to be asked. They have learned the methodological lesson; now they can use the shortcut without falling into the error it previously would have caused.
refashioned:
The lesson the Stranger draws from this comic result: the dialectical method does not care about prestige. It treats the king and the bird-catcher with the same logical rigor, arriving at truth regardless of what flatters human self-importance. This principle was already demonstrated in the Sophist, and it holds here too.

Having made the methodological point, the Stranger now offers to take the shorter path to defining the king. The detour through the longer route was never about reaching the destination faster -- it was about learning how to divide properly. Now that the lesson is internalized, they can use the shortcut without committing the error it would have produced before.
elegant: The dialectical method is no respecter of persons. It places the king beside the bird-catcher with the same indifference it would show to any other logical result. Having learned this lesson through the long route, they can now take the short one -- not because the detour was wasted, but because it taught them how to cut properly.
summary: The Stranger draws the methodological lesson: dialectical division follows logical structure impartially, without regard to social status. Having demonstrated this through the longer route, he now takes the shorter path to the definition, since the lesson about proper division has been learned.
distilled: The dialectical method is no respecter of persons -- it follows the logic of classification wherever it leads, indifferent to what flatters human importance.
notes: The reference to the Sophist confirms the dramatic continuity between the dialogues. In the Sophist (227a-b), the Stranger made a similar point about the method of division treating noble and ignoble arts with equal seriousness. The phrase 'no respecter of persons' anticipates a concept that became important in Jewish and Christian theology (prosopolepsia in the New Testament), though Plato's point is purely methodological. The decision to now take the 'shorter road' shows that the methodological detour served a pedagogical purpose: the long route taught Young Socrates how to divide properly, making the shortcut safe to use.

narrative: The Stranger now takes the shorter route. They should have begun by dividing land animals into bipeds and quadrupeds. Since only the winged herd (birds) shares the biped class with humans, they then divide bipeds into feathered and featherless. This immediately isolates the management of humankind. Once this art is brought to light, the time has come to install the Statesman in his place, like a charioteer receiving the reins of state -- for this vocation belongs to him.
refashioned: The shorter route makes the final definition efficient. Land animals divide into bipeds and quadrupeds. Among bipeds, the feathered (birds) separate from the featherless (humans). This isolates the management of humankind as a distinct art. The Statesman can now be installed in his position -- like a charioteer taking the reins -- as the person whose knowledge entitles him to direct human affairs.
elegant: Bipeds divide into feathered and featherless. Once birds are separated from humans, the art of managing humankind stands alone. The Statesman takes the reins like a charioteer -- his knowledge is his claim to authority.
summary: Taking the shorter route, the Stranger divides bipeds into feathered and featherless, cleanly isolating the management of humankind. The Statesman is compared to a charioteer receiving the reins of state.
distilled: Statesmanship is the art of managing featherless bipeds -- which is to say, the science of directing human collective life.
notes: The charioteer metaphor connects to the famous chariot allegory in the Phaedrus (246a-254e), where the soul is compared to a charioteer driving two horses. The 'featherless biped' definition became one of the most discussed (and mocked) definitions in the history of philosophy. Diogenes Laertius (VI.40) reports that Diogenes the Cynic responded to this definition by plucking a chicken and presenting it as 'Plato's man,' leading to the amended definition 'featherless biped with flat nails.' The quick division demonstrates what the longer route taught: how to cut at natural joints efficiently.

narrative: Young Socrates is satisfied: the Stranger has paid his debt and completed the argument. He jokes that the earlier digression on method was like interest added to the principal. The Stranger accepts the compliment but redirects: now they should go back to the beginning and assemble the full chain of divisions into a single connected definition of the Statesman's art.
refashioned: The argument appears complete, and Young Socrates is pleased -- he jokes that the methodological detour was like interest on a debt. But the Stranger wants to consolidate. Before moving forward, they should retrace the entire chain of divisions from beginning to end, linking every step into a single continuous definition.
elegant: The definition has been reached through a long chain of cuts. Now the links must be joined together into one continuous statement -- the full definition of the Statesman's art, from first principle to final distinction.
summary: With the division complete, the Stranger proposes going back to the beginning and assembling all the divisions into a single connected definition of the Statesman's art.
distilled: A definition is not complete until every step in the chain of divisions has been joined into a single continuous statement.
notes: Young Socrates' joke about 'interest' (tokos) on a 'debt' reflects the commercial metaphors common in Greek philosophical discourse. The term tokos literally means 'offspring' and was the standard Greek word for interest on a loan. The reference to the Republic in the editorial note connects to Republic 506e-507a, where Socrates similarly discusses paying intellectual debts. The practice of summarizing a chain of divisions into a synthetic definition is standard in Platonic dialectic -- the Sophist (268c-d) concludes with a similar summary.

narrative: The Stranger recites the complete chain. The science of pure knowledge had a part concerned with command. From this derived the art of command-for-self, as distinct from the relay of others' commands. An important section of self-command was the management of living animals, which was further limited to the management of herds. Within herds, the focus narrowed to pedestrian animals, then to those without horns, then to pure-bred animals that do not cross-breed. The only further subdivision is the art of man-herding -- the management of bipeds. This is what they have been seeking, and it turns out to be at once the royal and political art.
refashioned: The full definition, assembled link by link: Begin with all knowledge. Divide it into theoretical and practical. Within theoretical knowledge, divide into judging and commanding. Within commanding, divide into delegated and sovereign command. Sovereign command over living beings divides from command over lifeless objects. Living beings divide into individual care and herd management. Herds divide into water and land. Land herds divide into flyers and walkers. Walkers divide into horned and hornless. Hornless divide into cross-breeding and pure-bred. Pure-bred divide into quadrupeds and bipeds. The management of bipeds -- humans -- is the art of the Statesman. This is the royal and political science.
elegant: Knowledge divides into theory and practice, theory into judgment and command, command into delegated and sovereign, sovereign into dominion over the living and the lifeless, the living into individuals and herds, herds into water and land, land into flying and walking, walking into horned and hornless, hornless into cross-bred and pure, pure into four-footed and two-footed. At the end of this chain stands the Statesman: the sovereign commander of the human herd.
summary: The complete chain of divisions: from all knowledge through theoretical, commanding, sovereign, living, herding, land, walking, hornless, pure-bred, to biped -- arriving at the art of managing humankind, which is the royal and political science.
distilled: The Statesman is defined as the sovereign intellectual commander of the collective life of featherless, pure-bred, hornless, walking, land-dwelling, tame, gregarious, living beings.
notes: This summary recapitulates every division made since 2.1, compressing approximately twelve binary divisions into a single synthetic definition. The technique of recapitulation is characteristic of Platonic method -- the Sophist (268c-d) ends with a similar chain. The compound definition 'shepherding pure-bred animals' (eilikrinous zootrophia) uses a term (eilikrines) meaning 'pure, unmixed, genuine' that has philosophical resonance in Plato -- it appears in the Phaedo (66a) describing the soul's desire for pure knowledge unmixed with bodily influence. The identification of the royal art with the political art reaffirms the premise from 2.1.

narrative:
Young Socrates thinks the investigation is complete. But the Stranger is not satisfied. He suspects they have described a rough outline of the royal form but have not yet worked out the true image of the Statesman in his own nature. The definition seems adequate on the surface, but something essential is missing.

The Stranger identifies the problem. The argument defined the Statesman as one type of shepherd among many -- the one who rears humans collectively rather than horses or cattle. But here is what distinguishes the king from all other shepherds: no rival challenges the cattle-herder's claim to be the sole manager of his herd. Yet the Statesman faces countless rivals. Merchants, farmers, food-providers, trainers, and physicians all claim to rear and manage humankind. These claimants insist they care not only for the common populace but for the rulers themselves. Are they not right to make this claim?
refashioned:
Young Socrates thinks they have finished, but the Stranger sees a fundamental problem. The definition identifies the Statesman as the shepherd of the human herd -- but unlike every other herdsman, the Statesman has rivals.

No one challenges the cattle-herder's exclusive claim over his herd. But the moment you call the Statesman a 'shepherd of humans,' a crowd of competitors appears: merchants who supply the people's goods, farmers who grow their food, doctors who tend their health, trainers who develop their bodies, teachers who shape their minds. Every one of these professions claims to manage human welfare. Some even claim to care for the rulers themselves. If the Statesman is merely the herdsman of humanity, how does he differ from all these other providers and caretakers?
elegant: No one disputes the cattle-herder's authority over his herd. But call the Statesman a shepherd of humans, and every merchant, farmer, doctor, and teacher steps forward to claim the same title. The definition has rivals, which means it is not yet precise enough.
summary: The Stranger identifies a fundamental flaw in the definition: unlike other herdsmen, the Statesman faces rivals. Merchants, farmers, doctors, and teachers all claim to manage human welfare, making the 'shepherd of humanity' definition too broad to isolate the Statesman's unique role.
distilled: A definition that cannot exclude its rivals has not yet found what it is looking for.
notes: This passage marks the critical turning point of the dialogue's first movement. The herdsman analogy, which has structured the entire inquiry so far, is now revealed to be inadequate. The list of rivals -- merchants (emporoi), farmers (georgoi), food-providers (sitopoinoi), trainers (gymnastai), and physicians (iatroi) -- represents the major functional roles in a Greek city-state. The objection anticipates Aristotle's analysis in Politics III.4-5 of the relationship between the statesman and the various experts who contribute to civic life. It also connects to the Republic's discussion of the division of labor in the just city (369b-374d).

narrative:
The Stranger sharpens the contrast. Consider any ordinary herdsman -- a shepherd of cattle, for instance. No one disputes his comprehensive authority over his herd. He is the sole feeder, physician, and matchmaker of his animals. He is their midwife, their musician, their comforter. He consoles them with his voice and his instruments as far as their nature permits. No one else claims any of these functions.

But the Statesman enjoys no such undisputed monopoly. Every one of these functions has its own specialist claiming authority over human beings. The shepherd analogy, which seemed to define the Statesman perfectly, actually obscures the crucial question: what exactly does the Statesman do that no one else can claim to do?
refashioned:
The ordinary herdsman is the undisputed master of every aspect of his flock's existence. He feeds them, heals them, breeds them, delivers their young, entertains them, and soothes them. No one challenges his comprehensive authority.

The Statesman has no such monopoly. For every function the herdsman performs alone, there is a human specialist claiming jurisdiction: the doctor heals, the farmer feeds, the trainer conditions, the merchant supplies, the priest consoles. The shepherd analogy breaks down precisely because human societies have division of labor. The question the definition has failed to answer is: what does the Statesman do that all these specialists cannot?
elegant: The shepherd feeds, heals, breeds, comforts, and entertains his flock with undisputed authority. The Statesman does none of these things exclusively -- each function has its own specialist. The herdsman analogy fails because human life is too complex for any one person to manage comprehensively.
summary: The ordinary herdsman has undisputed authority over every aspect of his flock's care. The Statesman, by contrast, faces specialists who claim each of those functions -- feeding, healing, training, and comforting. The shepherd analogy breaks down because human societies involve division of labor.
distilled: The herdsman analogy fails because no single person can monopolize the care of human beings the way a shepherd monopolizes the care of sheep.
notes: The Stranger's description of the herdsman as feeder, physician, matchmaker, midwife (maiotikon), and musician draws on the realities of ancient pastoral life. Shepherds did in fact serve all these functions for their flocks. The recognition that the herdsman analogy fails for human governance represents a significant philosophical advance over the Homeric tradition of the 'shepherd of the people.' The argument from division of labor parallels the Republic's foundational argument for the city (369b-c): the city arises because no individual is self-sufficient, and each person does best at one specialized task. Here the same principle is turned against the analogy that was supposed to define the ruler.

narrative:
Young Socrates concedes the point. The Stranger asks directly: can their argument about the king be true and unimpeachable? Were they right to select him from ten thousand rivals as the shepherd and rearer of the human flock? Surely not. They may have described a rough outline of the royal form, but they have not accurately worked out the true image of the Statesman. To reveal him as he truly is, they must disengage him from all those who crowd around him claiming to share his prerogatives. They cannot let the argument end in disgrace by leaving this problem unresolved.

Young Socrates agrees -- the work must continue. The Stranger proposes a new beginning: they will travel by a different road. He suggests introducing a famous tale, weaving a good portion of it into the argument, and then resuming their series of divisions to arrive at the definition they seek. The inquiry is far from over.
refashioned:
The definition has failed its own test. Calling the Statesman a 'shepherd of humanity' does not distinguish him from the crowd of merchants, doctors, farmers, and teachers who all claim to manage human welfare. The argument cannot end here without disgracing itself.

The Stranger proposes starting over on a different road. Rather than continuing the chain of divisions directly, he will introduce a myth -- a famous tale that will illuminate what the shepherd analogy got wrong and point toward a better definition. The inquiry is far from finished; the real work of defining the Statesman has only just begun.
elegant: The shepherd analogy has been tried and found insufficient. The Statesman cannot be defined by what he shares with every other caretaker of human beings -- he must be defined by what separates him from them all. A myth will illuminate where the divisions went wrong, and then the inquiry will begin again on firmer ground.
summary: The Stranger acknowledges that the definition of the Statesman as a shepherd is insufficient because it cannot distinguish him from rival claimants. He proposes a new approach: a mythological tale that will illuminate the error, followed by a fresh series of divisions.
distilled: When a definition cannot exclude its rivals, the inquiry must start over -- not by repeating the same steps, but by finding an entirely different road.
notes: The 'famous tale' the Stranger is about to introduce is the Myth of Cronus (or the Cosmic Myth), which will occupy the next section of the dialogue. The decision to use myth at this point is methodologically significant: when the method of division reaches an impasse, Plato introduces narrative as a different kind of philosophical tool. This parallels the use of myths in the Republic (the allegory of the cave, the Allegory of Er), the Phaedrus (the chariot myth), and the Timaeus (the cosmological myth). The phrase 'ten thousand rivals' (myrioi amphisbetountes) uses the Greek word for an indefinitely large number, emphasizing the severity of the problem. The transition from division to myth marks the end of the dialogue's first major movement.
